title: Public `_burn` lets anyone destroy arbitrary LAND and corrupt balances
claim: `_burn` is declared `public` and only checks the caller-supplied `from` and `owner` arguments for equality. It never verifies `msg.sender`, token existence, or that `owner` matches the real owner resolved by `_ownerOf`/`_ownerAndOperatorEnabledOf`. Any external account can therefore call `_burn(victim, victim, id)` and overwrite arbitrary `_owners[id]` slots with the burn sentinel while decrementing any chosen address balance.
impact: An attacker can permissionlessly burn live LAND parcels belonging to other users, permanently tombstone unminted 1x1 ids or internal quad-layer storage slots so they can never be minted/regrouped, and underflow arbitrary `_numNFTPerAddress` balances by burning nonexistent ids. This can destroy user assets, brick future supply, and corrupt accounting.
paths: Call `_burn(alice, alice, childId)` for a live LAND tile owned by Alice; the function sets `_owners[childId] = 2**160` and decrements Alice's balance without any authorization check.
Call `_burn(target, target, nonexistentId)`; because there is no existence check, `_numNFTPerAddress[target]--` executes and can underflow if the target balance is too small.
Call `_burn(victim, victim, LAYER_3x3/6x6/12x12/24x24 + baseId)` to tombstone internal quad slots, causing future mint/regroup logic that expects zeroed storage to fail permanently.

title: Burning one child LAND permanently breaks transfers of enclosing quads
claim: Burning sets `_owners[id] = 2**160` as a sentinel. `LandBaseToken._ownerOf()` casts that value down to `address(0)`, so burned children look nonexistent to ownership queries. But regrouping uses `_checkAndClear()`, which treats any nonzero raw storage value as an owned slot and requires `address(owner) == from`. For the burn sentinel this becomes `address(2**160) == from`, i.e. `address(0) == from`, which can never hold for a valid owner.
impact: After any authorized burn of a 1x1 parcel inside a 3x3/6x6/12x12/24x24 region, later `transferQuad` or `batchTransferQuad` calls that need to regroup that enclosing area revert forever. A single burned tile can therefore permanently lock high-value quads from being transferred as quads and force owners into fragmented management of the remaining parcels.
paths: A user owns a 24x24 quad and burns one child tile via `burn(id)` or `burnFrom(from, id)`.
Later the owner calls `transferQuad(from, to, 24, x, y, data)` or any enclosing `12`, `6`, or `3` regroup.
During `_regroup*`, `_checkAndClear()` encounters the burned child, sees a nonzero storage slot, and reverts with `not owner`, blocking the quad transfer permanently.

title: Quad mints and quad transfers can silently lock LAND in contracts that lack the custom batch receiver interface
claim: `mintQuad`, `transferQuad`, and `batchTransferQuad` only perform receiver validation when the destination is a contract that both answers ERC165 within the hard-coded 10,000 gas budget and claims support for the custom `ERC721MandatoryTokenReceiver` interface. Contracts that implement only standard `onERC721Received`, omit ERC165, or have an expensive `supportsInterface` are treated as if no callback is required, so the quad transfer/mint succeeds without any acceptance check.
impact: Users can mint or transfer up to 576 LAND tiles into contracts that cannot handle or release them, permanently locking the assets. Because the quad APIs have no strict safe variant that enforces receiver acceptance, callers cannot reliably force on-chain validation before moving an entire quad.
paths: A minter calls `mintQuad` to a contract that is not an EOA but does not expose ERC165 support for `ERC721MandatoryTokenReceiver`; the mint succeeds without any callback.
A user calls `transferQuad` or `batchTransferQuad` to a marketplace/vault that only implements standard ERC721 receiving logic; the transfer bypasses the receiver check and strands the LAND.
A receiver contract does implement ERC165, but its `supportsInterface` path exceeds the 10,000 gas budget; `_checkInterfaceWith10000Gas` returns false and the protocol skips acceptance checks anyway.
